APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it addresses a core NLP task (SRL) of broad interest, evaluates on standard benchmarks (CoNLL-2005/2012) with accepted metrics (F1, predicate correctness), and compares to strong prior work. The methodology uses contemporary deep-RNN training advances (deep highway BiLSTMs, recurrent dropout, orthonormal initialization), constrained A* decoding, and ensembles, with hyperparameters reported and code released, matching the empirical and reproducibility expectations I prioritize. The paper also provides thoughtful analysis of strengths/limitations (long-distance dependencies, syntactic signal) and thorough related work coverage, placing it squarely within the venue’s community. | B(5): This paper is squarely within the scope of a top-tier NLP venue: it advances core SRL methodology, uses standard CoNLL-2005/2012 benchmarks, and compares to strong prior work (Zhou & Xu, FitzGerald, Täckström, Toutanova). The problem formulation (BIO tagging with constrained A* decoding), rigorous evaluation (F1, predicate-level correctness), and ablations of architectural choices (highway BiLSTMs, recurrent dropout, orthonormal init) meet community standards. Its analysis of long-distance dependencies, implicit syntax learning, and remaining failure modes (PP attachment, adjuncts, constraint violations) is precisely the kind of linguistically informed error analysis that will interest both SRL and broader NLP researchers. Given these points and released code, the paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue.

The methodology uses contemporary deep-RNN training advances (deep highway BiLSTMs, recurrent dropout, orthonormal initialization), constrained A* decoding, and ensembles, with hyperparameters reported and code released, matching the empirical and reproducibility expectations I prioritize. The paper also provides thoughtful analysis of strengths/limitations (long-distance dependencies, syntactic signal) and thorough related work coverage, placing it squarely within the venue’s community.

The problem formulation (BIO tagging with constrained A* decoding), rigorous evaluation (F1, predicate-level correctness), and ablations of architectural choices (highway BiLSTMs, recurrent dropout, orthonormal init) meet community standards. Given these points and released code, the paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper applies a set of recently developed training techniques (deep highway BiLSTMs, recurrent dropout, orthonormal initialization) and constrained A* decoding to SRL and shows strong empirical gains; these are valuable engineering and empirical contributions but not a fundamentally new idea. The problem formulation is well-studied (CoNLL SRL), and the authors clearly differentiate their work from prior syntax-based and syntax-free systems while providing code and hyperparameters. Overall the techniques constitute a non-trivial, well-executed combination of existing methods rather than a novel methodological breakthrough, so the contribution is moderately novel. | B(4): The paper does not introduce a wholly new problem formulation (it addresses established span-based SRL) but presents a notable and clearly differentiated methodological contribution: applying recent deep-RNN training advances (8-layer highway BiLSTMs with recurrent dropout and orthonormal initialization) combined with constrained A* decoding and a product-of-experts ensembling strategy to push state-of-the-art. These techniques represent a non-trivial engineering and modeling advance over prior SRL work (Zhou & Xu; FitzGerald et al.), and the analysis claiming implicit learning of constituency and improved long-distance dependency recovery would be informative and somewhat surprising to experts. Overall the work is a substantial, well-differentiated incremental advance rather than a radical new direction.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies a set of recently developed training techniques (deep highway BiLSTMs, recurrent dropout, orthonormal initialization) and constrained A* decoding to SRL and shows strong empirical gains; these are valuable engineering and empirical contributions but not a fundamentally new idea. IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses an important, practical problem (span-based SRL) and reports substantial, reproducible gains over strong baselines (83.2/83.4 F1 on CoNLL-2005/2012) using well-specified engineering choices (deep highway BiLSTM, recurrent dropout, orthonormal init, constrained A* decoding) and released code. From my empirical-methods perspective, the work is likely to change practitioner practice by showing which deep-RNN training and decoding techniques matter, and it credibly demonstrates that explicit syntax is not strictly necessary while still helpful—opening clear follow-up studies. It is therefore a strong-impact contribution (not fully transformative) that others will build on for sequence-tagging and SRL research. | B(4): This paper addresses a central problem (accurate SRL) and demonstrates that applying modern deep-RNN training techniques plus constrained decoding and ensembling yields substantial, reproducible gains over prior syntax-heavy and syntax-free systems (83.2/83.4 F1 on CoNLL benchmarks). Its careful analyses showing strong long-distance recovery and implicit learning of constituency structure will change how researchers weigh explicit syntactic features versus powerful sequence models, and the released code/hyperparameters increases likelihood of adoption. While impactful and likely to redirect subsequent SRL work and tooling, it stops short of overturning the role of syntax entirely (explicit syntax still helps), so I rate its influence as strong but not fully transformative.

From my empirical-methods perspective, the work is likely to change practitioner practice by showing which deep-RNN training and decoding techniques matter, and it credibly demonstrates that explicit syntax is not strictly necessary while still helpful—opening clear follow-up studies. It is therefore a strong-impact contribution (not fully transformative) that others will build on for sequence-tagging and SRL research.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early, including the highway BiLSTM, constrained A* decoding, and ensembling results. Notation and methodological details appear consistent and reproducibility is supported (code release and reported hyperparameters), which aids clarity for replication. Figures, tables, and examples are informative and the writing is generally good, though a few experimental-design choices (e.g., which decoding constraints were tested and their impact) could be presented with slightly more explicit ablation narrative to aid readers in following cause–effect. | B(4): The paper is well organized and follows a clear logical flow from motivation through methods, experiments, and conclusions, and its main contributions (deep BiLSTM with constrained A* decoding and gains on CoNLL-2005/2012) are stated early. Notation and methodological details appear consistent and supported by reported hyperparameters and public code, and related work coverage is thorough. Figures, tables, and examples are generally effective, though a few discussions (e.g., which constraints hurt performance and why) could be clarified further for readers seeking implementation guidance. Overall the writing is good with only minor presentation issues that prevent an exceptional rating.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early, including the highway BiLSTM, constrained A* decoding, and ensembling results. Notation and methodological details appear consistent and reproducibility is supported (code release and reported hyperparameters), which aids clarity for replication. reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized and follows a clear logical flow from motivation through methods, experiments, and conclusions, and its main contributions (deep BiLSTM with constrained A* decoding and gains on CoNLL-2005/2012) are stated early. Notation and methodological details appear consistent and supported by reported hyperparameters and public code, and related work coverage is thorough. Figures, tables, and examples are generally effective, though a few discussions (e.g., which constraints hurt performance and why) could be clarified further for readers seeking implementation guidance. Overall the writing is good with only minor presentation issues that prevent an exceptional rating.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments are strong: evaluation on CoNLL-2005 and CoNLL-2012 with competitive baselines, reported hyperparameters, released code, and targeted analyses (long-range dependencies, effect of enforcing SRL constraints). The paper also reports limitations and negative results (e.g., enforcing R roles can hurt), and it includes component-level claims (highway BiLSTMs, recurrent dropout, orthonormal init, constrained A* decoding, ensembling) that appear to be evaluated. My main residual concerns — not addressed in the summary — are statistical robustness (variance across random seeds, significance tests) and whether detailed ablations isolate each architectural/training trick quantitatively; these are minor gaps given the overall thoroughness. | B(4): The paper provides strong empirical evidence across standard benchmarks (CoNLL-2005 and CoNLL-2012) and compares to relevant baselines, showing large gains from the proposed deep BiLSTM, constrained A* decoding, and ensembling. Related work appears well surveyed, hyperparameters and code are available, and the authors analyze failure modes (adjunct/PP attachment, constraint violations) and syntactic behavior, which supports their claims. However, from the summary it is not fully clear that thorough ablation studies isolate the contribution of each architectural/training advance versus decoding and ensembling, so a minor gap remains in sensitivity analysis. Overall the experimental treatment is solid and reproducible but could better quantify each component's impact.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are strong: evaluation on CoNLL-2005 and CoNLL-2012 with competitive baselines, reported hyperparameters, released code, and targeted analyses (long-range dependencies, effect of enforcing SRL constraints). The paper also reports limitations and negative results (e.g., enforcing R roles can hurt), and it includes component-level claims (highway BiLSTMs, recurrent dropout, orthonormal init, constrained A* decoding, ensembling) that appear to be evaluated. My main residual concerns — not addressed in the summary — are statistical robustness (variance across random seeds, significance tests) and whether detailed ablations isolate each architectural/training trick quantitatively; these are minor gaps given the overall thoroughness.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides strong empirical evidence across standard benchmarks (CoNLL-2005 and CoNLL-2012) and compares to relevant baselines, showing large gains from the proposed deep BiLSTM, constrained A* decoding, and ensembling. Related work appears well surveyed, hyperparameters and code are available, and the authors analyze failure modes (adjunct/PP attachment, constraint violations) and syntactic behavior, which supports their claims. However, from the summary it is not fully clear that thorough ablation studies isolate the contribution of each architectural/training advance versus decoding and ensembling, so a minor gap remains in sensitivity analysis. Overall the experimental treatment is solid and reproducible but could better quantify each component's impact.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper compares against a broad and relevant set of prior SRL systems (Zhou & Xu 2015; FitzGerald et al. 2015; Täckström et al. 2015; Toutanova et al. 2008; Punyakanok et al. 2008; Pradhan et al. 2013) on the standard CoNLL-2005 and CoNLL-2012 benchmarks using F1 and percent-correct metrics, which supports fair apples-to-apples evaluation. It also reports hyperparameters, releases code, and analyzes contributions of specific components (deep highway BiLSTMs, recurrent dropout, orthonormal init, constrained A* decoding, ensembling), and explicitly acknowledges where prior or syntax-informed methods remain competitive or complementary. From a reproducibility and baselines perspective the comparisons are comprehensive and well-justified. | B(5): The paper evaluates on standard CoNLL-2005 and CoNLL-2012 benchmarks using F1 and predicate-complete metrics and compares to a broad set of recent and classic baselines (e.g., Zhou & Xu 2015; FitzGerald et al. 2015; Täckström et al. 2015; Toutanova et al. 2008), covering both syntax-aware and syntax-free approaches. Comparisons appear fair (same data and metrics), the authors report hyperparameters and release code, and they explicitly acknowledge when syntax helps or when constrained decoding can hurt, indicating transparent treatment of competing methods. From my SRL-focused perspective the coverage and analysis are comprehensive and the paper situates its contributions clearly relative to relevant prior work.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a broad and relevant set of prior SRL systems (Zhou & Xu 2015; FitzGerald et al. 2015; Täckström et al. 2015; Toutanova et al. 2008; Punyakanok et al. 2008; Pradhan et al. 2013) on the standard CoNLL-2005 and CoNLL-2012 benchmarks using F1 and percent-correct metrics, which supports fair apples-to-apples evaluation. It also reports hyperparameters, releases code, and analyzes contributions of specific components (deep highway BiLSTMs, recurrent dropout, orthonormal init, constrained A* decoding, ensembling), and explicitly acknowledges where prior or syntax-informed methods remain competitive or complementary. From a reproducibility and baselines perspective the comparisons are comprehensive and well-justified.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates on standard CoNLL-2005 and CoNLL-2012 benchmarks using F1 and predicate-complete metrics and compares to a broad set of recent and classic baselines (e.g., Zhou & Xu 2015; FitzGerald et al. 2015; Täckström et al. 2015; Toutanova et al. 2008), covering both syntax-aware and syntax-free approaches. Comparisons appear fair (same data and metrics), the authors report hyperparameters and release code, and they explicitly acknowledge when syntax helps or when constrained decoding can hurt, indicating transparent treatment of competing methods. From my SRL-focused perspective the coverage and analysis are comprehensive and the paper situates its contributions clearly relative to relevant prior work.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by empirical results on CoNLL-2005 and CoNLL-2012 with strong baselines, reported hyperparameters, and released code, showing large gains from deep highway BiLSTMs, constrained A* decoding, and ensembling. There are no nontrivial mathematical derivations to check. My main methodological concern is that the summary does not report variability across random seeds or statistical significance testing, and it’s unclear whether comprehensive ablations isolate each training advance (highway connections, recurrent dropout, orthonormal init) versus other factors. These are relatively minor and do not overturn the results, but they limit confidence in which components drive the improvements. | B(4): Overall the paper’s experimental claims are well supported: it reports clear gains on CoNLL-2005 and CoNLL-2012, compares to relevant baselines (Zhou & Xu, FitzGerald et al., Täckström et al.), and ablates key engineering choices (highway BiLSTM, recurrent dropout, orthonormal init, constrained A* decoding, ensembling). There are no mathematical derivations to check, and the methodology appears sound, though the summary does not mention statistical significance testing. My main technical reservations are modest: the claim that deep models learn constituency implicitly would benefit from stronger, more direct evidence, and the discussion that enforcing some SRL constraints hurts performance suggests complex data issues that deserve deeper analysis; these do not, however, undermine the principal experimental findings.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by empirical results on CoNLL-2005 and CoNLL-2012 with strong baselines, reported hyperparameters, and released code, showing large gains from deep highway BiLSTMs, constrained A* decoding, and ensembling. There are no nontrivial mathematical derivations to check. My main methodological concern is that the summary does not report variability across random seeds or statistical significance testing, and it’s unclear whether comprehensive ablations isolate each training advance (highway connections, recurrent dropout, orthonormal init) versus other factors. These are relatively minor and do not overturn the results, but they limit confidence in which components drive the improvements.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper’s experimental claims are well supported: it reports clear gains on CoNLL-2005 and CoNLL-2012, compares to relevant baselines (Zhou & Xu, FitzGerald et al., Täckström et al.), and ablates key engineering choices (highway BiLSTM, recurrent dropout, orthonormal init, constrained A* decoding, ensembling). There are no mathematical derivations to check, and the methodology appears sound, though the summary does not mention statistical significance testing. My main technical reservations are modest: the claim that deep models learn constituency implicitly would benefit from stronger, more direct evidence, and the discussion that enforcing some SRL constraints hurts performance suggests complex data issues that deserve deeper analysis; these do not, however, undermine the principal experimental findings.

main_claims: A deep highway BiLSTM model with constrained A* decoding substantially improves state-of-the-art span-based SRL, achieving 83.2 F1 on CoNLL-2005 and 83.4 F1 on CoNLL-2012 (ensemble).

Applying recent deep-RNN training advances (highway connections, recurrent dropout, orthonormal initialization) plus constrained decoding and ensembling yields large gains over prior syntax-based and syntax-free systems.

Deep models excel at recovering long-distance predicate-argument dependencies and can learn syntactic constituency structure implicitly, but explicit syntactic information can still further improve performance.
_summary.methodology: Treat SRL as a BIO tagging problem and use an 8-layer highway bidirectional LSTM with recurrent dropout and orthonormal initialization, decode with constrained A* search enforcing BIO and selected SRL/syntactic constraints, and ensemble via a product-of-experts over models.
limitations: Model still makes errors on adjunct distinction and prepositional-phrase (PP) attachment.
Predictions can violate SRL-specific constraints; enforcing some constraints (R/reference roles) can hurt performance because they are commonly violated in gold data.
Although deep models learn long-range dependencies well, performance degrades for arguments farther from the predicate and explicit syntactic input can still provide improvements.

reviewer_a.name: Empirical Methods Engineer
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with a PhD in NLP focusing on reproducible deep-learning experiments and engineering of sequence-tagging systems.
traits: insists on strong, well-matched baselines and clear implementation details (hyperparameters, training curves, compute budget)
values comprehensive ablations to attribute gains to specific components (e.g., highway connections vs recurrent dropout vs decoding constraints)
checks statistical validity and robustness (variance across seeds, significance tests, error bars)
looks for reproducibility artifacts: code release, dataset splits, random seeds, and exact evaluation scripts
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I assess whether the experimental design convincingly supports the claims and whether others could reproduce and build on the results.

reviewer_b.name: Semantic NLP Strategist
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher in semantic parsing and information extraction with extensive experience evaluating the impact of modeling advances on downstream NLP pipelines and linguistic analysis.
traits: examines novelty and contribution relative to prior SRL and syntax-aware approaches
values analysis of linguistic behavior (long-distance dependencies, implicit syntax learning) and relevance to downstream tasks
looks for clear discussion of limitations, generalizability, and how the work shifts research directions
appreciates human-readable error analyses and comparisons that illuminate where gains come from
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I judge the paper by how it advances understanding and practice for SRL and whether its insights will meaningfully influence future research and applications.
